coredata.dc:title: A double-edged effect of manganese-doped graphene quantum dots on salt-stressed Capsicum annuum L.
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Yuqing
Landa, Elizabeth Noriega
Cantu, Jesus M.
Hernandez-Viezcas, Jose A.
Nair, Aruna Narayanan
Lee, Wen-Yee
Sreenivasan, Sreeprasad T.
Gardea-Torresdey, Jorge L.
coredata.dc:description: The objective of the current study is to evaluate both the positive and negative effects of manganese-doped graphene quantum dots (GQD-Mn) on Capsicum annuum L. grown under salt stress. GQD-Mn was synthesized, characterized, and foliar-applied (250 mg/L, 120 mg/L, 60 mg/L) to C. annuum L. before and after the flowering stage, during which 100 mM of NaCl solution was introduced into the soil as salt stress. Controls were designed as absolute control (no nanomaterials or salt) and negative control (no nanomaterials only salt). Herein, we report that GQD-Mn offset the reduction of fruit production in salt-stressed C. annuum L. by around 40 %. However, based on a comprehensive analysis of normal alkanes (n-alkane) using gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS), we also observed that the leaf epicuticular wax profile was disturbed by GQD-Mn, as the concentration of long-chain n-alkanes was increased. Meanwhile, the content of magnesium (Mg) and zinc (Zn) indicated a potential promoted photosynthesis activity in C. annuum L leaves. We hypothesize that the optical properties of GQD-Mn allow leaves to utilize light more efficiently, thus improving photosynthetic activities in plants to acclimate salt stress. But the increased light usage also induced heat stress on the leaf surfaces, which caused n-alkanes changes. Our results provided a unique perspective on nano-plant interaction that value both beneficial and toxic effects of nanomaterials, especially when evaluating the safety of nano-enabled agriculture in areas facing harsh environmental conditions such as salinity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene nanomaterials
Plant
Light usage
Heat stress
Epicuticular wax

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene quantum dots preparation and characterization
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and electrophoretic light scattering (ELS)
Optical properties analysis by UV–Vis spectroscopy
High dispersive Mn-doped graphene quantum dots
GQD-Mn's optical properties and potential in increasing the light usage for plant leaves
GQD-Mn alleviates fruit reduction and promote leaf photosynthesis under salinity
GQD and GQD-Mn disturbed plants epicuticular wax profile like heat stress
n-Alkanes abundance and chain composition changes

National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Yuqing Ye and Jorge Gardea-Torresdey conceptualized the investigation and planned the experiments. Yuqing Ye, Elizabeth Noriega Landa, Jesus M. Cantu, and Jose A. Hernandez-Viezcas conducted the research and investigation process. Wen-Yee Lee, Sreeprasad T. Sreenivasan, and Jorge Gardea-Torresdey contributed to the visualization and supervision. Yuqing Ye prepared the original draft. Elizabeth Noriega Landa, Jesus M. Cantu, Jose A. Hernandez-Viezcas, Aruna Narayanan Nair, Wen-Yee Lee, Sreeprasad T. Sreenivasan, and Jorge L. Gardea-Torresdey reviewed and edited the manuscript. Wen-Yee Lee, Sreeprasad T. Sreenivasan, and Jorge Gardea-Torresdey acquired the financial support.

The authors acknowledge the National Science Foundation and the Environmental Protection Agency under Cooperative Agreement Number DBI-1266377. Any opinions, findings, and conclusions or recommendations expressed in this material are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the views of the National Science Foundation or the Environmental Protection Agency. This work has not been subjected to EPA review and no official endorsement should be inferred. Partial funding was provided by the NSF ERC on Nanotechnology-Enabled Water Treatment ( EEC-1449500 ). This work was also supported by Grant 2G12MD007592 from the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (NIMHD), a component of the National Institutes of Health (NIH). W-Y. L. and E. N. L acknowledge the National Institute of General Medical Sciences of the National Institutes of Health under award number SC1CA245675 and U54MD007592 respectively. J. L. G-T acknowledges the Dudley family for the Endowed Research Professorship and the University of Texas System's 2018 STARs Retention Award.

The authors acknowledge the National Science Foundation and the Environmental Protection Agency under Cooperative Agreement Number DBI-1266377. Any opinions, findings, and conclusions or recommendations expressed in this material are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the views of the National Science Foundation or the Environmental Protection Agency. This work has not been subjected to EPA review and no official endorsement should be inferred. Partial funding was provided by the NSF ERC on Nanotechnology-Enabled Water Treatment (EEC-1449500). This work was also supported by Grant 2G12MD007592 from the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (NIMHD), a component of the National Institutes of Health (NIH). W-Y. L. and E. N. L acknowledge the National Institute of General Medical Sciences of the National Institutes of Health under award number SC1CA245675 and U54MD007592 respectively. J. L. G-T acknowledges the Dudley family for the Endowed Research Professorship and the University of Texas System's 2018 STARs Retention Award.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQD and GQD-Mn; (A) SEM image of dried droplet of GQD-Mn suspension on a silicon wafer. (B) Hydrodynamic sizes and ζ-potential of GQD and GQD-Mn in DI water.

Excitation-emission maps for photoluminescence from GQD in DI water (A1), GQD-Mn in DI water (B1), GQD in diluted plant extract (A2), and GQD-Mn in diluted plant extract (B2). The Z scale bars in the figures indicate the photoluminescence intensity (a.u). Special emission from GQD and GQD-Mn are marked in black circles in all four maps. Two blue dashed circles in A2 and B2 showing the extra emission potentially from plant molecules in plant extract. Rayleigh scattering has been masked in grey in all four maps for clarity.

C. annuum L. fruit production and photosynthesis-involved elements analysis. (A) Fruit number, (B) Fruit total weight, (C) Mg content in leaves, and (D) Zn content in leaves. In all four figures, absolute control (grey clear bar), negative control (grey striped bar), GQD treated groups (red gradient bar), GQD-Mn treated groups (blue gradient bar), and GQD-Mn with salt stress treated group (blue gradient striped bar). In absolute control, plants were exposed to no nanomaterials or salt, and in negative control, plants were exposed to no nanomaterials but only salt. Letters represent groups of independent variables in ANOVA analysis (p ≤ 0.05).

Analysis of leaf wax n-alkanes. (A) n-alkanes abundance in different treatments. The * represents a significant difference when compared with the absolute control (p ≤ 0.05). (B) Ternary diagram of n-alkanes composition in different treatments. (C) Average chain length of n-alkanes in different chain length range.

head.ce:title: A double-edged effect of manganese-doped graphene quantum dots on salt-stressed Capsicum annuum L.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, The University of Texas at El Paso, 500 West University Avenue, El Paso, TX 79968, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The University of Texas at El Paso

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, The University of Texas at El Paso, 500 West University Avenue, El Paso, TX 79968, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Science and Engineering Ph.D. program, The University of Texas at El Paso, 500 West University Avenue, El Paso, TX 79968, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Environmental Science and Engineering Ph.D. program
The University of Texas at El Paso

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental Science and Engineering Ph.D. program, The University of Texas at El Paso, 500 West University Avenue, El Paso, TX 79968, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, The University of Texas at El Paso, 500 West University Avenue, El Paso, TX 79968, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The University of Texas at El Paso

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The objective of the current study is to evaluate both the positive and negative effects of manganese-doped graphene quantum dots (GQD-Mn) on Capsicum annuum L. grown under salt stress. GQD-Mn was synthesized, characterized, and foliar-applied (250 mg/L, 120 mg/L, 60 mg/L) to C. annuum L. before and after the flowering stage, during which 100 mM of NaCl solution was introduced into the soil as salt stress. Controls were designed as absolute control (no nanomaterials or salt) and negative control (no nanomaterials only salt). Herein, we report that GQD-Mn offset the reduction of fruit production in salt-stressed C. annuum L. by around 40 %. However, based on a comprehensive analysis of normal alkanes (n-alkane) using gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS), we also observed that the leaf epicuticular wax profile was disturbed by GQD-Mn, as the concentration of long-chain n-alkanes was increased. Meanwhile, the content of magnesium (Mg) and zinc (Zn) indicated a potential promoted photosynthesis activity in C. annuum L leaves. We hypothesize that the optical properties of GQD-Mn allow leaves to utilize light more efficiently, thus improving photosynthetic activities in plants to acclimate salt stress. But the increased light usage also induced heat stress on the leaf surfaces, which caused n-alkanes changes. Our results provided a unique perspective on nano-plant interaction that value both beneficial and toxic effects of nanomaterials, especially when evaluating the safety of nano-enabled agriculture in areas facing harsh environmental conditions such as salinity.

ce:section.ce:para: Nano-enabled agrochemicals are products that contain nanomaterials to enhance crop production while maintaining ecological integrity (Okeke et al., 2022). They are promising ways to solve agricultural challenges such as limited arable lands, over-population, and biotic or abiotic stresses (White and Gardea-Torresdey, 2018). Currently, salinity is one of the most severe abiotic stressors inhibiting plant development at different stages (Liang et al., 2018). Genetic engineering can improve salt tolerance in crops; however, public concerns are rising about their potential toxicity and allergenicity to human beings (Kumar et al., 2020). Therefore, nanotechnology has gained increased interest as it has shown positive effects in promoting growth and alleviating salt stress in crops (Ye et al., 2020; Saffie-Siebert et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2018; Cherati et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020). However, in the meantime, nanoagrochemials are normally applied through root or leaf exposure, during which they tend to accumulate in environmental media such as water, soil, and air, or translocate into crop edible tissues, thus, causing potential toxicity in living beings during food consumption. Therefore, it is also vital to understand the nano-plant interaction during nanomaterial application, particularly, the interface between leaf and nanomaterials as foliar application is the most popular agricultural practice (Avellan et al., 2021).

Nanomaterials are reported to alleviate salt stress in crops through various mechanistic ways. For example, cerium-based nanoparticles (CeNPs) scavenged reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Wu et al., 2018) as they entered plant cells, thus, altering the downstream signaling and maintaining the cytosolic K+/Na+ balance (Liu et al., 2021). Moreover, CeNPs shortened the root apoplastic barrier, which reduced the capacity of salt in roots by allowing the transportation of excess Na+ from roots to shoots (Rossi et al., 2017). Silicon (Si) based-NPs was formulated to deliver flavonoid quercetin in salt-stressed textile hemp foliarly (250 mM NaCl, 60 mL × 4 times) and was found to induce higher level of sucrose and decreased expression of stress-response genes. Quercetin is a stress-mitigating compound in plants and therefore was proposed to serve as biostimulants to relieve salt stress symptoms in hemp (Saffie-Siebert et al., 2021). Nevertheless, plant essential elements-based NPs are rarely explored in plant stress management. We recently reported that manganese (Mn) NPs (0.1–1.0 mg/L) (Ye et al., 2020) reduced salt stress (100 mM NaCl, 5 mL germination media) induced symptoms without triggering oxidative stress in C. annuum L. seedlings, such as offseting root elongation reduction by around 55 % and promoting antioxidant expression up to 1.8 fold. This study (Ye et al., 2020) also showed the translocation of MnNPs into the interior seed coat and proposed that the positive effects were due to the dissolved Mn2+ from MnNPs because Mn is an essential micronutrient and its ionic counterparts can alleviate abiotic stresses in plants (Ye et al., 2019). Conversely, advanced imaging techniques have assisted in revealing the nano-plant interaction and determined five potential routes for nanomaterials' uptake (stomata, cuticle, trichomes, hydathodes, and necrotic spots) (Avellan et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2020). To date, stomata and hydathodes are known as major physical openings for NPs internalization, and few studies suggested the direct uptake of hydrophobic NPs through plants cuticle (Schreck et al., 2012; Larue et al., 2014a; Larue et al., 2014b; Avellan et al., 2019). The cuticle is a protective layer composed of cutin and cuticular wax (intracuticular and epicuticular wax), which are polyesters and a mixture of diverse hydrocarbons, respectively (Buschhaus and Jetter, 2011). In cuticle, the intracuticular wax is structurally embedded in cutin while epicuticular wax sticks out to the atmosphere as wax crystals. This unique morphology protects leaves from water loss, strong UV radiation, pathogens, and pests (Avellan et al., 2021). Therefore, the biosynthesis of those hydrocarbons in cuticular wax is typically influenced by environmental conditions such as water availability and temperature (Andrae et al., 2019).

Although the morphology of leaf wax makes the cuticle the first barrier during nanomaterial's exposure, the role of the cuticle in nano-leaf interactions is underexplored, and little is known about the nano-triggered effects in cuticular wax synthesis, such as the length and distribution of the hydrocarbons in the wax. In addition, the studies of carbonaceous nanomaterials in plant stress management are generally lacking and our previous reported MnNPs is limited in dispersity and Mn2+ efficiency. Therefore, to address these gaps, we selected natural derived graphene quantum dots (GQD) as a highly dispersive carrier for Mn2+. We hypothesized that the carried Mn2+ will alleviate salt stress induced symtoms in C. annuum L., and the unique optical properties of GQD-Mn will promote the light usage of chlorophyll molecules and enhance the photosynthesis, thus help in offseting salt stress in C. annuum L.. We not only investigated whether GQD-Mn assisted C. annuum L. in combating salt stress, but also we performed the analysis of n-alkanes in order to reveal the GQD-Mn's influence on C. annuum L. leaves. This effort yielded a comprehensive analysis of n-alkanes between n-C9 and n-C35. The abundance and composition of long-chain n-alkanes responded the most to the application of both salt stress and GQD-Mn, similar to plants' natural responses toward other desiccating environments (Andrae et al., 2019). Our work is the first to report the alternations in leaf wax n-alkane profile under graphene nanomaterial exposure, highlighting both the beneficial potential and risk of nanomaterials in current agricultural practice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots preparation and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of both GQD and GQD-Mn were performed through hydrothermal method using lignin as precursor, as described earlier (Nair et al., 2020) and in the supplementary information. Briefly, the hydrothermal reaction conditions were optimized to achieve the best yield, both GQD and GQD-Mn were lyophilized at the end and stored in powder form for further characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and electrophoretic light scattering (ELS)
ce:section.ce:para: The size and structure of powder GQD and GQD-Mn were analyzed and validated in our previous and current study,(Nair et al., 2020) FTIR spectrometer (Agilent Cary 630) was used to confirm the aromatic CC and oxygen-containing functional groups on the edges of GQD and GQD-Mn. To further fulfill the needs of this study, we conducted hydrodynamic radius and ζ-potential measurement by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and electrophoretic light scattering (ELS) using a Malvern Panalytical Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Before the measurement, 50 mg/L suspensions were prepared by suspending GQD and GQD-Mn powder with Milli-Q water, followed by sonication in ice-bath for 15 min (Crest Ultrasonics 275DA). All measurements were conducted with three replicates using a laser (λ = 633 nm, θ = 90°) and particle refractive index of n = 2.42.

The GQD-Mn was diluted to 10 ppm with Milli-Q water for scanning electron microscopy imaging. A sample volume of 4 μL was pipetted onto a silicon wafer. The sample was air-dried for 5 min to allow the deposition of graphene nanomaterials. Then the sample was coated with gold for 40 s at a plasma current of 18 mA (SPI-Module sputter coater). SEM imaging was performed on a Hitachi S-4800 using an acceleration voltage of 15 KeV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties analysis by UV–Vis spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Two hundred μL of GQD and GQD-Mn (50 mg/L) were pipetted in a 96-well plate, followed by emission measurement at the excitation wavelength from 250 to 500 nm (Multi-mode microplate reader, Spectra Max M3). Then the same concentration of GQD and GQD-Mn suspension were prepared using diluted plant extract followed by the same procedure. The plant extract was prepared by grinding 10 g of fresh leaves in liquid nitrogen, followed by adding phosphate buffer (100 mM, 100 mL, pH = 7.4). Then the plant extract was centrifuged (5000 ×g), and the supernatant was spiked in DI water (1: 99, v:v) to make the diluted plant extract. All measurements were calibrated and conducted with 5 replicates.

C. annuum L. was cultivated between March to June 2021 at the environmental growth chamber of the University of Texas at El Paso, with an average temperature and humidity of 22.1 °C and 70 %, respectively. First, C. annuum L. seeds were disinfected and then planted in vermiculate, followed by transplanting the seedlings into growing pots after 3 weeks. Then the seedlings were cultivated for another 3 months, which including the flowering and fruiting stages. The nanomaterials (250 mg/L, 120 mg/L, 60 mg/L) were foliar applied to the plants before and after the flowering stage in a total of 3 times (when plants were 35, 55, 75 days old), each time 20 mL of nanomaterials suspension was sprayed on both sides of the leaves. However, in the later fruit production analysis, we merged three concentration into one as no significant difference was found in the individual application. The salt stress (100 mM NaCl, 100 mL) was introduced to the soil for the first time when the plants were 37 days old, and then 4 more times (when the plants were 57, 61, 65, and 69 days old). Controls were designed as absolute control and negative control, which represent plants were exposed to no nanomaterials or salt, and exposed to no nanomaterials but only salt, respectively. Last, all C. annuum L. were harvested from the growing pots.

After harvesting, C. annuum L. leaves were collected, followed by washing with Milli-Q water and 0.5 % HNO3. Then the leaves were oven-dried (72 °C, 72 h) and digested (20 mg/sample) with 4 mL of plasma pure HNO3 in the DigiPREP MS digestion block (SCP Science, NY) for 45 min at 115 °C. The digests were then cooled to room temperature and diluted to 50 mL with Milli-Q water. ICP–OES (PerkinElmer Optima 4300 DV) was used for the elemental analysis, in which blanks, spikes (SCP Science), standard reference materials (Peach leaves, NIST-SRM 1547, 96 % recovery), and analytes' calibration curves were used for quality control and quality assurance.

Chemical analysis of n-alkanes in the leaf wax was performed in a thermal desorption unit (TD3.5+, Gerstel) coupled with Gas Chromatography/Mass Spectrometry (8890/5977B-N GC/MS, Agilent Technologies, Wilmington DE). The thermal desorption process was set under the splitless mode and programmed as follows. The initial temperature was set at 50 °C holding for 0.5 min; the temperature was ramped at 100 °C/min to 300 °C and held for 3 min. Desorption gas flow was set at 0.9 mL/min. During desorption, the compounds were concentrated in a cold injection system (CIS4, Gerstel) at −40 °C. Once the desorption process was completed, the CIS4 was heated to 300 °C at 12 °C/s and held for 1 min. Alkanes were separated and analyzed by GC–MS using solvent vent mode through a HP-5 ms Ultra Inert capillary column (30 m × 0.25 μm × 0.25 μm; Agilent J&W Columns). The oven temperature was programmed as follows. The temperature was initially set at 50 °C and held for 5 min, ramped to 200 °C at 10 °C/min and held for 1 min, increased to 260 °C/min at 3 °C/min, and finally heated to 310 °C/min at 5 °C/min and held for 5 min. The alkanes were detected by mass selective detector in scan mode (40–500 m/z). The National Institute of Standards and Technology Library (NIST17) was used for the identification of alkanes, calibration curve, quality control and quality assurance.

Analyses were done using SPSS and coordinated with the statistical package in OriginPro 2018, all figures were created using OriginPro 2018. For data analysis, the normality was tested through the Shapiro-Wilkinson test, and then followed by One-way ANOVA analysis and Tukey HSD test comparison (p ≤ 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: High dispersive Mn-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the morphology and dispersity of the synthesized GQD and GQD-Mn, we performed serial characterizations. In our previous study, GQD and GQD-Mn were both characterized as a 1 nm stack of graphene layers and with around 0.21 nm interplanar distance (Nair et al., 2020). Their average size were determined by TEM as 10 nm (Nair et al., 2020). Besides, the graphene structure and oxygen-contained functional groups on the edges of GQD and GQD-Mn were spectroscopically confirmed in the previous study (Nair et al., 2020), which explained their high dispersity in water. We further imaged a dried droplet of GQD-Mn using SEM, and the image showed a well-dispersed thin layer of circular sheets (10–15 nm) on silicon wafer with some of the edges connected or overlapped. (Fig. 1A.) Excellent dispersity in water makes GQD-Mn as a good candidate for further foliar application. We also investigated the diffusion pattern of GQD and GQD-Mn in water. It showed that GQD and GQD-Mn diffuse as particles with diameters of 122.3 ± 0.8 and 96.3 ± 0.5 nm, respectively (Fig. 1B). In addition, under pH = 5, GQD-Mn carries thick electrical double layer with a surface charge of −21.2 mV (Fig. 1B), which demonstrates the high possibility that graphene nanomaterials diffuse into plant leaves through stomatal pores other than the cuticular pathway during the foliar application process (Hu et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD-Mn's optical properties and potential in increasing the light usage for plant leaves
ce:section.ce:para:
We further obtained the optical properties of GQD and GQD-Mn suspension (50 mg/L) in water and diluted plant extract (1/100). The photoluminescence was measured as a function of excitation wavelength (λ
ex ) ranging from 250 to 500 nm, and its intensity was indicated as the Z scale (Fig. 2 ). Our efforts yielded four heat maps representing graphene nanomaterials' characteristic emissions (λ
em ) without Rayleigh scattering, which is expected to have the highest intensity and was erased in grey (Fig. 2). Both GQD and GQD-Mn have strong emissions around 380–460 nm under excitation of 320–390 nm (Fig. 2, A1 and B1, black circles), and the two noticeable bands localized around 330 nm and 380 nm are due to π-π* transition of the carbon system and n-π* transitions of the groups on graphene edges, respectively (Nair et al., 2020). However, GQD-Mn has lower intensity of emission due to the emission-suppressing effect from Mn2+ quenching (Supplementary Fig. 1) (Tan et al., 2011). Interestingly, the λ
em of GQD and GQD-Mn lies within the absorbance spectra of plant molecules such as chlorophyll a and b. We predicted that the GQD and GQD-Mn, which were foliar applied on plant leaf, would increase the plant light usage because they provide additional photons particularly involved in photosynthesis. We then introduced plant molecules by spiking the phosphate extract in graphene nanomaterials' suspensions at a volume ratio of 1 to 99. This dilution step avoided photoluminescence signals of GQD and GQD-Mn being masked by plant's fluorescence. We observed that the emissions of GQD and GQD-Mn (λ
em , 380–460 nm) were significantly reduced in the presence of plant extract, which are marked in black circles on heat maps (Fig. 2, A2 and B2). This reduction verified the possibility of chlorophyll absorbing photons of 380–460 nm that were transferred by GQD and GQD-Mn. Additionally, compared to Fig. 2A1 and B1, the blue dashed circles showed additional emission around 450 nm (λ
ex
> 380 nm), further validating the presence of plant molecules and their fluorescence induced by available photons (λ
ex
> 380 nm) from the incident light and graphene nanomaterials' photoluminescence. We could not show the complete spectra of plant fluorescence in Fig. 2A2 and B2 due to the cutoff of the Rayleigh scattering, but the absorption-emission was later confirmed with only plant extract present (Supplementary Fig. 2). Therefore, we propose that the presence of GQD and GQD-Mn on leaf surfaces can potentially improve the leaf light usage due to their special optical properties. And the efficient light usage of leaves led to enhanced photosynthetic activities in plants that help them to acclimate salt stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD-Mn alleviates fruit reduction and promote leaf photosynthesis under salinity
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we examined whether GQD-Mn can alleviate the salt stress in C. annuum L., we performed a concentration-independent analysis, in which we combined all three concentrations (60, 120, 250 mg/L) of the nanomaterials since no important significant difference was found in the individual application (Supplementary Fig. 3). Compared with the absolute control, salt stress significantly reduced fruit production by 97 ± 0.0 % (fruit number) and 98 ± 0.98 % (total weight), but GQD-Mn offset the reduction (41 ± 0.55 % and 58 ± 0.8`3 %, fruit number and total weight, respectively), though GQD-Mn did not show any positive effects on C. annuum L. growth under non-stressed condition. (Fig. 3A and B). This is interesting when, compared with our previous study (Ye et al., 2020), manganese nanoparticles (MnNPs) not only alleviated root length reduction under salt stress bur also promoted C. annuum L. root elongation in non-stressed condition. The different effects of GQD-Mn and MnNPs on C. annuum L. growth under non-stressed condition is partly due to Mn ions' availability; as we earlier reported that supplementary Mn2+ significantly promote C. annuum L. growth (Ye et al., 2020). It could be rationalized by the fact that very limited Mn ions can be provided by GQD-Mn (Nair et al., 2020), while MnNPs can dissolve into abundant Mn ions after few hours of suspending in water (Ye et al., 2020). As GQD did not show any adverse effect on C. annuum L.(Fig. 3A and B), the insufficient supply of Mn2+ from GQD-Mn was attributed as the reason for the non-promoted growth. Meantime, we noticed an interesting, though non-statistically significant decrease performance of GQD-Mn compared to GQD in non-stressed conditions. We considered the optical properties of GQD and GQD-Mn might dominate this phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 2A1 and B1, higher emission allows GQD to provide more light source for plant leaves than GQD-Mn and presumably increase the fruit growth.

The alleviation effect of GQD-Mn on salt-stressed C. annuum L. demonstrated its potential in plant stress management. Graphene was reported to alleviate salinity and alkalinity stresses by regulating plant's gene expression (Chen et al., 2021). It is plausible that GQD-Mn exhibited the same properties as graphene due to the same sp2 hybridization of their carbon. Furthermore, it might be a synergistic effect for GQD-Mn because supplementary Mn2+ ions are reported to combat abiotic stresses (Ye et al., 2019). In general, carbonaceous based nanomaterials have been shown to minimize the adverse effect of abiotic stresses on plants, by promoting the nutrition and defense system (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), scavenging reactive oxygen species (Li et al., 2021), or improving Ca2+ signaling and plant environmental adaptability (Li et al., 2022). Further investigations on biomolecular analysis are needed for GQD-Mn treated plants.

However, for the first time, we also attribute the alleviation effect to an enhanced light usage by chlorophyll molecules due to GQD-Mn application (Fig. 2). In this enhancement, the light promotes the photosynthesis process, which is normally severely affected when plants are under salt stress (Chaves et al., 2009). This hypothesis was further confirmed (Fig. 3C and D) by the analysis of two important photosynthesis-involved elements, magnesium (Mg) and zinc (Zn). Mg is the metal center of chlorophyll molecules, which can be excited by photons and serve as an electron donor in electron transport chain during photosynthesis (Levitt, 1954). Interestingly, compared to the absolute control, salt stress induced a significant increase in leaf Mg content, indicating a high demand for Mg in leaves, presumably for enhancing photosynthetic activity to combat salt stress (Fig. 3C). However, with the application of GQD-Mn, leaf Mg content stay statistically the same as the absolute control, regardless of the presence of NaCl (Fig. 3C). The steady Mg content is most likely caused by sufficient photosynthetic activity resulting from GQD-Mn application. In the same figure, we also noticed that without salt stress, the application of GQD and GQD-Mn did not reduce the Mg content in C. annuum L. leaves. This is consistent with our previous study, where we found nanomaterials only start showing beneficial properties when plants encounter stresses (Ye et al., 2020). We also observed a significant reduction of leaf Zn content in all nano-treated groups (Fig. 3D), while salt stress did not trigger any change. Zn is essential in plants' photosynthesis and respiration, as it activates certain enzymes and helps to produce chlorophyll. The reduction of Zn in C. annuum L. leaves indicated decreased needs for plants-responsive adjustments, as the graphene nanomaterials readily provided more available photons to promote photosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD and GQD-Mn disturbed plants epicuticular wax profile like heat stress
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the interaction interface between graphene nanomaterials and C. annuum L. leaves, we conducted GC–MS analysis to determine the concentrations of n-alkanes in the epicuticular wax profile. Typically, the epicuticular wax performs as outer shells of cuticles, and is the first barrier when leaf encounter the graphene nanomaterials. The epicuticular wax is composed of mixed normal alkanes (n-alkanes), and its chemical combinations are highly sensitive to environmental stimulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: n-Alkanes abundance and chain composition changes
ce:section.ce:para: We first assessed the abundance and composition of n-alkanes in the leaf epicuticular wax. The n-alkanes determined in all samples were from n-nonane (C9H20) to n-pentatriacontane (C35H72), noted as short-chain (C9-C21), mid-chain (C22-C27), and long-chain (C28-C35) of n-alkanes. Their abundance was recovered as μg/g of dry leaf using both internal and calibration standards (Fig. 4A). Compared to the absolute control (white bars), four of thirteen short-chain n-alkanes (C10H22, C15H32, C18H38, and C20H42) and two of eight long-chain n-alkanes (C28H58 and C33H68) had significantly shift in their abundance in response to graphene nanomaterials or salt stress exposure. We highlighted them as yellow in Fig. 4A for clarity. However, different exposure triggered slight difference in those alternations. For example, the salt stress only induced a spike in C33H68 but GQD additionally changed the abundance in C10H22, C15H32 and C18H38, while GQD-Mn only altered C18H38 and C20H42. The changes in short-chain n-alkanes may be due to unknown mechanisms involving the nano-bio interaction and the biosynthesis of leaf wax. Plants have a complicated pathway, with multiple signaling proteins and downstream enzymes responding to environmental changes (Ye et al., 2019; Kan et al., 2022). The GQD and GQD-Mn exposure may lead to the up or down-regulating in plants' certain genes, thus, controlling proteins/enzymes to promote or inhibit the biosynthesis of n-alkane chains. Carbonaceous nanomaterials have been previously reported to induce transcriptomic responses in plants facing environmental changes (Cherati et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). Meantime, though graphene sheets have been previously reported as “nano-blade” (Malina et al., 2019; Selim et al., 2020), we theoretically excluded the possibility of mechanical cutting of graphene layers on n-alkane molecules. Furthermore, the increased abundance of C33H68 in the negative control group is consistent with previously reported studies on environmental-stressed plants (Lewandowska et al., 2020; Kan et al., 2022), where the increased biosynthesis of very-long-chain n-alkanes contributing to the total cuticular wax content, in order to reduce water loss and herein to combat the salt stress. Interestingly, we did not find significant changes in the total cuticular wax content among different groups, but we observed altered carbon chain compositions in different groups, which also align with the previous studies (Lewandowska et al., 2020; Kan et al., 2022) and will be discussed further below in Fig. 4B. Additionally, the abundance of mid-chain n-alkanes is not visible in Fig. 4A but was reported in supplementary data. No significant changes were determined in the mid-chain n-alkanes' abundance among different groups. Overall, the abundance shifts in the limited number of short, mid, and long-chain n-alkanes are likely due to the harvest time of C. annuum L. When the plant reached the full maturity, the biosynthesis of n-alkanes remained constant on fully expanded leaves (Tipple et al., 2013). We predict that a real-time nano-exposure study on the leaf wax in the future can reveal more information.

Though the total n-alkane abundance is statistically the same among different groups, we determined the chain composition in n-alkanes of each sample and produced a ternary graph (Fig. 4B). Most data are presented at the low region of the graph, representing all samples are composed of a relative low percentage of mid-chain n-alkanes. This is consistent with the low abundance in mid-chain n-alkanes analyzed in Fig. 4A. We also observed that when C. annuum L. was exposed to GQD, GQD-Mn, and GQD-Mn with salt stress, the data accumulate mostly in the left patch (green). While the right patch (pink) majorly contains data from the absolute control and negative control groups. These right-to-left (pink to green in Fig. 4B) shifts represents a decreasing percentage of short chain n-alkanes and an increasing percentage of long-chain n-alkanes within each sample. The dominance of certain chains of n-alkanes in plants is responsive to plant species, growth stages, climate change, and environmental factors. It is well documented that n-alkane composition typically shifts to longer chain length in response to warm and dry climate, as they facilitate the epicuticular wax to be the primary barrier against water loss (Maffei et al., 1993; Bush and McInerney, 2013; Wang et al., 2015). Interestingly, the current compositions shifts were significantly induced by graphene nanomaterials instead of salt stress, though GQD-Mn group with salt stress also altered the shift, which presumably due to the synergic effect of GQD-Mn and salt stress. We hypothesize that both the GQD and GQD-Mn caused heat stress to the plant leaves because they provided extra energy by converting photons from 330–370 nm to 370–450 nm due to the optical properties of GQD and GQD-Mn (Fig. 2) and the latter can be further highly absorbed by chlorophyll and β-carotene molecules in the leaves (Supplementary Fig. 2). In addition, excited chlorophyll molecules can return to the ground state by releasing energy through heat dissipation, photosynthesis, and fluorescence (Colombo et al., 2017), which causes the potential heat stress and increased photosynthesis activity, as discussed earlier.

Furthermore, we investigated the average chain length (ACL) in each length region (Short-chain ACL9 – 21, mid-chain ACL22 – 27, and long-chain ACL28 – 35) of n-alkanes (Fig. 4C). ACL is a weighted average of carbon chain length (Eq. (1)) (Bush and McInerney, 2013); it considers the importance of each n-alkane thus it is a representative index for chain length change. The sediment preserved n-alkane ACL has been widely used as a proxy indicator to reconstruct past vegetation like grasslands and forests, as the former naturally prefer longer ACL than the latter. Meantime, ACL can also be used to reconstruct climate conditions as plants develop higher ACL in warm/dry than in cold/humid environments.
(1)
ACL =
∑
Cn ∗ n
∑ Cn

In Fig. 4C, we determined that long-chain ACL28 – 35 is the most sensitive index in response to different treatments. For example, the absolute control has the significantly lowest chain length among all groups, as if it were grown in the coldest or most humid environment. Since temperature, light, and humidity were all kept consistent, the high ACL28 – 35 value in other groups (negative control, GQD, GQD-Mn, and GQD-Mn with stress) are attributed to the external stressors such as high salinity, graphene nanomaterials exposure, and the combination of both. Thus, we confirmed our previous hypothesis that both GQD and GQD-Mn induced heat stress in C. annuum L. due to the higher ACL in the long-chain region. However, neither salt stress nor GQD exposure altered the ACL in short-chain region (Fig. 4C); this indicates that ACL cannot directly reflect the stress impact on plants, where the chain length region must be taken into consideration.

Interestingly, like earlier interpretations in Fig. 4A and B, we could not determine any significant difference in mid-chain ACL22 – 27, we concluded that mid-chain n-alkanes are the least sensitive group of n-alkanes in response to the treatments in this experiment. Overall, the n-alkanes analysis has shown the alternations in the abundance, composition, and ACL mostly happening in long-chain n-alkanes, which is similar and consistent with previous studies in environmentally stressed plants.

The designed graphene nanomaterials are highly dispersive, and possess unique optical properties that promote light usage in the photosynthesis of C. annuum L. leaves, and thus, offset the adverse effects caused by salt stress. This can be a promising material for large scale of application, because the carbon precursor of GQD-Mn is readily aquired lignosulfonate, and the synthesis route is straight forward. However, the optical properties of the graphene materials also induced heat stress on C. annuum L. leaves and caused the disturbance in epiticular wax profile. Moving forward, many questions need to be investigated in nano-plant interaction. For example, various real-time measurements in plants under nano-exposure are needed, such as photosynthetic parameters, enzymatic activities, and transcriptomic changes. Also, nanomaterials in-situ tracking can be highly informative but still challenging. Additionally, our study revealed that graphene nanomaterials showed a simultaneous positive and negative effect, as it helped with fruit growth under salt stress, but disturbed the natural epicuticular wax profile in a way similar to heat stress. However, other nano-plant interaction studies have been linked to either beneficial or toxic aspects of nanomaterials' exposure. Our study establishes a step toward critical evaluation of nanomaterials' safety in nano-enabled agriculture. The integration of well understanding the interaction mechanisms and the nanomaterials' physicochemical properties will undoubtedly improve the usage of nanotechnology in the future, particularly in areas with climate conditions such as salt, drought, and heat stresses.

Yuqing Ye and Jorge Gardea-Torresdey conceptualized the investigation and planned the experiments. Yuqing Ye, Elizabeth Noriega Landa, Jesus M. Cantu, and Jose A. Hernandez-Viezcas conducted the research and investigation process. Wen-Yee Lee, Sreeprasad T. Sreenivasan, and Jorge Gardea-Torresdey contributed to the visualization and supervision. Yuqing Ye prepared the original draft. Elizabeth Noriega Landa, Jesus M. Cantu, Jose A. Hernandez-Viezcas, Aruna Narayanan Nair, Wen-Yee Lee, Sreeprasad T. Sreenivasan, and Jorge L. Gardea-Torresdey reviewed and edited the manuscript. Wen-Yee Lee, Sreeprasad T. Sreenivasan, and Jorge Gardea-Torresdey acquired the financial support.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the National Science Foundation and the Environmental Protection Agency under Cooperative Agreement Number DBI-1266377. Any opinions, findings, and conclusions or recommendations expressed in this material are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the views of the National Science Foundation or the Environmental Protection Agency. This work has not been subjected to EPA review and no official endorsement should be inferred. Partial funding was provided by the NSF ERC on Nanotechnology-Enabled Water Treatment (EEC-1449500). This work was also supported by Grant 2G12MD007592 from the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (NIMHD), a component of the National Institutes of Health (NIH). W-Y. L. and E. N. L acknowledge the National Institute of General Medical Sciences of the National Institutes of Health under award number SC1CA245675 and U54MD007592 respectively. J. L. G-T acknowledges the Dudley family for the Endowed Research Professorship and the University of Texas System's 2018 STARs Retention Award.

ACS Nano

Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf.
